1: sēper tôlĕdōt ʾādām: "This is the scroll/document of the generations of Adam." Sēper (H5612, scroll, document, book) is the first use of the word "book" in the Bible — this genealogy is presented as a written record, not merely an oral tradition. Tôlĕdōt (H8435, generations/account) is the structural marker of Genesis (Genesis 2:4; Genesis 5:1; Genesis 6:9; etc.); it introduces each major section. The verse then recapitulates Genesis 1:26-27: God created humanity (ʾādām) in the likeness (dĕmût, H1823) of God. The repetition anchors the genealogy in the creation mandate — these are the offspring of the image-bearers.
2: zākār ûnĕqēbāh bĕrāʾām waybārek ʾōtām: "Male and female he created them, and he blessed them." The creation binary (zākār ûnĕqēbāh) and the blessing are restated from Genesis 1:27-28 at the head of the post-fall genealogy, signaling that the image and blessing remain despite the expulsion. Wayyiqrāʾ ʾet-šĕmām ʾādām: "And he named them 'Mankind'" — God names the human pair collectively with the generic noun, tying individual identity to species-identity in a way that makes Adam's fall genuinely corporate.
3: bĕdamûtô kĕṣalmô: "In his own likeness, after his own image." The reversal from v1-2 is subtle but deliberate: creation says God made Adam in God's image and likeness; here Adam begets Seth in Adam's image and likeness. The same vocabulary (ṣelem + dĕmût) is used in both directions. The implication is that the image transmitted through human generation is now the image of fallen Adam, not the pristine image of Genesis 1:26 — though not destroyed, it is now refracted through a fallen bearer. This is the philological basis for the Reformed doctrine of original sin's transmission through natural generation.
4: wayyihyû yĕmê-ʾādām ʾaḥărê hôlîdô ʾet-šēt šĕmōneh mēʾôt šānāh: "The days of Adam after he fathered Seth were 800 years." The formulaic structure of Genesis 5 — birth age of key son, remaining years, total, "and he died" — is the basic unit of the genealogy. The function of wayyāmōt (and he died) is not merely chronological; it is the structural response to Genesis 2:17 ("you will surely die"). Each entry in the genealogy is a case study in the execution of the death sentence announced in the garden. Adam lives 930 years — long, but finite.
5: wayyāmōt: "And he died." The three-word refrain at the end of each genealogical entry (wayyihyû kol-yĕmê [name] ... šānāh wayyāmōt) functions as a liturgical reminder: the death sentence of 2:17 is inescapable. Adam, despite living 930 years, dies. The absence of this refrain for Enoch (v24: "and he was no more, for God took him") is structurally arresting — the exception that proves the rule and points forward to bodily resurrection as the ultimate exception.
6: wayyĕḥî šēt ḥămiš šānîm ûmĕʾat šānāh wayyôled ʾet-ʾĕnôš: "When Seth had lived 105 years, he fathered Enosh." ʾĔnôš (H583) derives from a root meaning weak, sick, or mortal — the same root that gives us the common noun for "mortal man" in Hebrew poetry (e.g., Psalms 8:4; Genesis 90:3). The name of the second generation after Adam means mortality itself — the genealogy is not optimistic about the trajectory of post-fall humanity.
7: wayyĕḥî-šēt ʾaḥărê hôlîdô ʾet-ʾĕnôš šebāʿ šānîm ûšĕmōneh mēʾôt šānāh: The formulaic structure continues. The long lifespans of the antediluvian genealogy have prompted several explanations: (1) genuine pre-flood longevity reflecting a different pre-flood environment; (2) compressed generational counts (years = short periods); (3) scribal transmission of an ancient kinglist tradition. Whatever their historical referent, within the narrative they convey an era of extended human vitality before the flood — the pre-curse creation still bearing fruit in bodily longevity even as it decays morally.
8: wayyāmōt: "And he died." Seth's 912 years end with the same refrain. The death formula is not varied or softened — each entry ends identically. The structural effect is cumulative: nine consecutive "and he died" entries (except Enoch) builds an inexorable rhetorical weight. Death is the universal conclusion to human biography in this chapter, broken only once.
9: wayyĕḥî ʾĕnôš tišʿîm šānāh wayyôled ʾet-qênān: "When Enosh had lived 90 years, he fathered Kenan." The name qênān (Kenan, H7018) may relate to the root qwn (to lament/wail) or to qānāh (acquire) — its derivation is uncertain. The Sethite genealogy proceeds without the cultural achievements attributed to the Cainite line (vv17-22): no cities, no music, no metallurgy — only births, years, and deaths. The Sethite line is characterized not by cultural accomplishment but by the continuity of the covenant seed.
10: wayyĕḥî ʾĕnôš ʾaḥărê hôlîdô ʾet-qênān: The formula continues. The pattern of lifespans in Genesis 5 is not random: most run between 777 and 969 years, with Enoch's 365 being notably shorter (and complete in a solar-year sense). The longevity is presented as the norm; the post-flood genealogy of Genesis 11 shows a systematic decrease toward the normal human lifespan, documenting the progressive enactment of the creation's groaning under the fall.
11: wayyāmōt: "And he died." Enosh dies after 905 years. The refrain accumulates. The reader of the Hebrew feels the grammatical finality of wayyāmōt — the waw-consecutive perfect (narrative past) form that terminates each biography. Death is not presented as natural or fitting; it is the sentence carried out. The genealogy is not celebrating longevity — it is documenting the execution of 2:17 across generations.
12: wayyĕḥî qênān šibʿîm šānāh wayyôled ʾet-mahălalʾēl: "When Kenan had lived 70 years, he fathered Mahalalel." Mahălalʾēl (H4111) means "praise of God" — the first clearly theophoric name in the Sethite genealogy. The pattern of names in Genesis 5 has been interpreted cumulatively as an encoded theological message (Adam = man; Seth = appointed; Enosh = mortal; Kenan = sorrow; Mahalalel = blessed God; Jared = shall come down; Enoch = teaching; Methuselah = his death shall bring; Lamech = powerful; Noah = comfort) — though speculative and disputed, the theophoric naming pattern is real.
13: wayyĕḥî qênān ʾaḥărê hôlîdô ʾet-mahălalʾēl: Kenan lives 840 additional years after fathering Mahalalel. The mathematical precision of the genealogy — exact ages at fathering, exact additional years, exact totals — reflects either historical record-keeping or a literary convention of precision to convey authenticity. The Mesopotamian king-lists use similar formulaic precision for antediluvian rulers with even longer reigns (Alulim of Eridu reigns 28,800 years in one tradition), suggesting a shared ANE convention for the deep past.
14: wayyāmōt: "And he died." Kenan's 910 years conclude with the refrain. The fourth death in the genealogy. The cumulative effect of the death refrain in Genesis 5 is to create what one commentator called a "monotonous minor key" — every biography, however long, ends on the same note. The genealogy is not triumphalist; it is brutally honest about the human condition under the sentence of Genesis 2:17.
15: wayyĕḥî mahălalʾēl ḥāmēš šānîm wĕšiššîm šānāh wayyôled ʾet-yāred: "When Mahalalel had lived 65 years, he fathered Jared." Yāred (H3382, Jared) may derive from yārad (to descend/come down). The structural placement of Jared as the father of Enoch (v18) — the man who walks with God and is taken by God — is significant: Jared parents the great exception to the death formula.
16: wayyĕḥî mahălalʾēl ʾaḥărê hôlîdô ʾet-yāred: The formula continues with Mahalalel living 830 additional years. The precision of the age-at-fathering figures enables chronologists to calculate the date of the flood from the creation (the "Anno Mundi" tradition of Ussher and others derives from exactly this arithmetic in Genesis 5 and 11). The genealogy functions as a chronological spine, not merely a name-list.
17: wayyāmōt: "And he died." Mahalalel's 895 years end with the refrain. The fifth death. The genealogy has now carried the death formula through five entries; by the time the reader reaches Enoch in v24, the structural interruption of the refrain is felt sharply. Each successive death makes the exception more striking.
18: wayyĕḥî-yāred šĕtayim wĕšiššîm šānāh ûmĕʾat šānāh wayyôled ʾet-ḥănôk: "When Jared had lived 162 years, he fathered Enoch." Ḥănôk (Enoch, H2585) means "dedicated" or "initiated" — the same name as Cain's son in Genesis 4:17, but in the Sethite line a different person. The parallel names in the Cainite and Sethite genealogies (Enoch, Lamech) have been explained as either parallel cultural traditions, deliberate literary contrast, or a compiler's acknowledgment of shared naming stock.
19: wayyĕḥî-yāred ʾaḥărê hôlîdô ʾet-ḥănôk šĕmōneh mēʾôt šānāh: The formula continues with Jared's remaining 800 years. Jared lives 962 years total — the second longest lifespan after Methuselah (969, v27) — and then dies with the formula. His son Enoch will break the pattern. The contrast is prepared gradually: the longest-lived fathers the shortest-lived (in years on earth).
20: wayyāmōt: "And he died." Jared's 962 years end. Six deaths recorded. The structural preparation for Enoch is now nearly complete — six entries with the death refrain, setting the stage for the seventh to be different. The number seven carries covenantal weight in Genesis (seven days of creation; Lamech's sevenfold vengeance); the seventh entry in the death sequence breaks the pattern.
21: wayyĕḥî ḥănôk ḥāmēš wĕšiššîm šānāh wayyôled ʾet-mĕtûšālaḥ: "When Enoch had lived 65 years, he fathered Methuselah." Mĕtûšālaḥ (Methuselah, H4968) is explained in various traditions as "man of the javelin" (mat + šelaḥ) or as "his death shall bring" (mût + šālaḥ). The second etymology, if correct, would make Methuselah's death the appointed signal for the flood — and the chronology of Gen 5 and 7 shows Methuselah dying in the year of the flood. Whether intended by the narrator is debated, but the name has been read as a divine calendar embedded in the genealogy.
22: wayyithallēk ḥănôk ʾet-hāʾĕlōhîm: "Enoch walked with God." The verb is hithallēk — the Hithpael (reflexive/intensive) stem of hālak (H1980, to walk). The Hithpael of hālak denotes a reciprocal, ongoing, habitual walking — not a single journey but a continuous mode of life. It is the same form used of Noah in 6:9 and of the expected behavior of covenant partners (Leviticus 26:12: "I will walk among you"). The phrase is not "walked before God" (as Abraham walks, Genesis 17:1) or "walked in the ways of God" but "walked with God" — language of intimate companionship, the closest OT description of the pre-fall state available to a post-fall person.
23: wayyihyû kol-yĕmê ḥănôk ḥāmēš wĕšiššîm šānāh ûšĕlōš mēʾôt šānāh: "All the days of Enoch were 365 years." Enoch's total lifespan (365 years) is the shortest in the pre-flood genealogy and equals the number of days in a solar year. Whether this is coincidence or deliberate symbolism is debated. What is clear is that 365 is dramatically less than his peers (Seth 912, Enosh 905, Methuselah 969); his earthly time is abbreviated not by death but by translation.
24: wayyithallēk ḥănôk ʾet-hāʾĕlōhîm wĕʾênennû kî-lāqaḥ ʾōtô ʾĕlōhîm: "Enoch walked with God, and he was no more, for God took him." The death refrain breaks: instead of wayyāmōt (and he died), the text says wĕʾênennû (and he was not/no more) — the same construction used in Genesis 37:30 (Joseph is "no more") and Jeremiah 31:15 (the children "are no more"). The verb lāqaḥ (H3947, to take) is the same verb used of Elijah's translation in 2 Kings 2:3,5,9,10 ("the LORD will take your master from over you"). The LXX translates wayyithallēk ḥănôk ʾet-hāʾĕlōhîm as "Enoch pleased God," which Hebrews 11:5 quotes directly ("By faith Enoch was taken up so that he should not see death"). The Hebrew says "walked with"; the LXX says "pleased" — both are legitimate interpretations of the idiom, and the NT uses the LXX reading to make the faith connection explicit.
25: wayyĕḥî mĕtûšālaḥ šebāʿ wĕšĕmōnîm šānāh ûmĕʾat šānāh wayyôled ʾet-lāmek: "When Methuselah had lived 187 years, he fathered Lamech." The formula resumes after Enoch's exceptional entry. Methuselah returns the genealogy to normalcy — his son Lamech will be Noah's father, and Methuselah will die in the year of the flood (if the etymology of his name is accepted). The genealogy is now building toward its climax in Noah.
26: wayyĕḥî mĕtûšālaḥ ʾaḥărê hôlîdô ʾet-lāmek: The formula continues with Methuselah's remaining 782 years, giving him a total of 969 (v27) — the longest lifespan in the genealogy. The narrator may intend irony: the man whose name may mean "his death shall bring [the flood]" lives longest. His endurance is a species of divine patience, giving humanity maximum time before judgment arrives.
27: wayyāmōt: "And he died." Methuselah's 969 years conclude with the refrain. This is the penultimate death formula; after Lamech's death (v31), the genealogy will conclude with Noah's birth — not his death. The oldest man in recorded Scripture dies with the same three-word sentence as every other. Age does not exempt from the sentence of Genesis 2:17; only translation (Enoch) and the covenant (Noah) offer alternatives.
28: wayyĕḥî-lāmek šĕtayim wĕšĕmōnîm šānāh ûmĕʾat šānāh wayyôled bēn: "When Lamech had lived 182 years, he had a son." The departure from the formula here is subtle: the text says "he had a son" (ben) rather than naming the son immediately, delaying the name for the interpretive comment in v29. The name-comment structure signals a significant naming — not merely a genealogical entry but a prophetic declaration.
29: wayyiqrāʾ ʾet-šĕmô nōaḥ lēmōr zeh yĕnaḥămēnnû: "He named him Noah, saying, 'This one will comfort us.'" Nōaḥ (H5146) and yĕnaḥămēnnû (he will comfort us) connect to the root nāḥam (H5162, to comfort/relent/sigh) — the same root used of God's regret in Genesis 6:6. Noah's name is the linguistic bridge between the Lamech prophecy and the divine response to wickedness. Lamech's prophecy anticipates rest from the curse of the ground (citing Genesis 3:17); Noah will bring this rest through the flood — not by removing the curse, but by preserving the covenant seed through judgment and into a new post-flood order (Genesis 8:21-22).
30: wayyĕḥî lāmek ʾaḥărê hôlîdô ʾet-nōaḥ: Lamech lives 595 additional years after Noah's birth — a post-fathering life somewhat shorter than his peers (Methuselah lives 782 additional years; Jared 800). The compressed trajectory may be incidental or may reflect the narrowing pre-flood period as judgment approaches. Lamech dies before the flood (5:31 gives his total as 777 years; the flood comes in Noah's 600th year, Genesis 7:6).
31: wayyāmōt: "And he died." Lamech's 777 years conclude the second-to-last individual biography in the genealogy. The number 777 carries obvious triple-seven symbolism in a book where seven is covenantally significant (7 days of creation, 7-fold vengeance, 70x7 forgiveness). Whether the narrator intends the numerical symbolism or is recording a genuine traditional lifespan is a question the text does not resolve, but the triple pattern is structurally present.
32: wayyôled nōaḥ ʾet-šēm ʾet-ḥām wĕʾet-yāpet: "And Noah fathered Shem, Ham, and Japheth." The genealogy's concluding verse shifts from the death formula to a birth formula — Noah's entry does not end with wayyāmōt here (his death is recorded in Genesis 9:29). Instead, the genealogy opens outward into the three sons who will populate the post-flood earth. Šēm (name/renown), Ḥām (hot/dark?), Yāpet (enlargement/beauty?) — the three sons whose names head the Table of Nations in Genesis 10. The genealogy ends not in death but in the covenant family that will survive judgment.
